figures.caption: Table 3: Acceptance length and throughput of TTS relative to DFlash on Qwen3.5-122B with update stride (S). While S=1 has the highest acceptance length, S=5 achieves the best throughput.

Figure 1: (a) Acceptance length (AL) for the LiveCodeBench dataset on Qwen3-8B with increasing output lengths for three speculators. Beyond a certain number of output tokens, the draft model fails to accurately approximate the target model and the AL becomes vanishingly low (too close to 1), yielding practically no benefits. (b) Our proposal, Test-Time Speculation (TTS), adapts the draft model in real-time to address this limitation and consistently improves acceptance lengths.
Table 9: Comparison of online speculator adaptation methods.
Figure 5: Acceptance Length of TTS versus DFlash for (a) AIME 2024 and (b) LiveCodeBench on Qwen3-8B. The improvement (%) increases sharply with output length as TTS refines the drafter.
Figure 2: Acceptance Length of four tasks using (a) DFlash, (b) EAGLE-3, and (c) PARD speculators on the Qwen3-8B model. It consistently reduces with increasing output generation lengths.
Table 6: HuggingFace identifiers for DFlash and EAGLE-3 paired with each target model.

Figure 3: Acceptance Length of four tasks using DFlash speculator on (a) Qwen3.5-35B, (b) Qwen3.635B, and (c) Qwen3.5-122B models. Acceptance length consistently reduces with increasing output generation lengths even when larger models are used.

Table 7: Acceptance length of TTS relative to DFlash on Qwen3-8B with the update stride S. The default S=1 achieves the largest improvement across benchmarks. Higher values of S reduce the improvement in acceptance length as the speculator is adapted less frequently.
Table 4: HuggingFace identifiers for benchmarks used in our evaluations, grouped by domain.
Table 5: Our selection of models spans parameter sizes from 4B to 122B and encompasses both dense and hybrid MoE architectures across the Qwen-3, Qwen-3.5, and Llama-3.1 model families.

Figure 6: (a) Acceptance Length (AL) of TTS on Qwen3-8B with optimization steps per round (K). Higher values of K degrade AL as the draft over-fits to the same training samples. (b) Cross-entropy of the EAGLE-3 draft on the Llama3.1-8B target’s Top−1 token. At higher lengths, EAGLE-3 diverges from the target distribution; TTS restores this divergence by adapting the draft in real-time.
Table 1: Acceptance length of TTS and DFlash across models and tasks. ∆(%) denotes the relative improvement of TTS over DFlash. TTS consistently achieves higher acceptance length.
Table 2: Acceptance length of EAGLE-3 (EGL-3) and TTS across models and tasks. ∆(%) denotes the relative improvement of TTS over EGL-3. TTS consistently achieves higher acceptance lengths.

Figure 7: Execution timeline of TTS with strided updates and asynchronous pipelining. Every S speculation rounds, a gradient update is dispatched to a side CUDA stream and overlaps with the next S−1 rounds on the main stream, keeping the update off the critical path.

Figure 4: Distribution entropy (in nats) for Llama3.1-8B (target) with EAGLE-3 (draft). (a) Target entropy decreases with output length, indicating the target becomes increasingly confident in its predictions. (b) Over the same range, the draft’s Top−1 token sharply diverges from the target. (c) This divergence persists regardless of whether the token appeared earlier or was previously unseen.
